questions.text: The process by which water continually changes its form and circulates between oceans, atmosphere and land is:
questions.explanation: The sun's heat causes evaporation of water into vapour.

questions.text: A terrarium is:
options: An artificial enclosure for keeping small house plants
questions.explanation: Our earth is like a giant terrarium as the same water that existed centuries ago still exists today.

questions.text: Which of the following is a major source of fresh water?
questions.explanation: Other sources include ponds, springs, and glaciers.

questions.text: Ocean water is salty because it contains large amounts of dissolved:
questions.explanation: Sodium chloride is the common table salt that we eat.

questions.text: Salinity is the amount of salt in grams present in ________ grams of water.
questions.explanation: The average salinity of the oceans is 35 parts per thousand.

questions.text: Which sea has a very high salinity (340 grams per litre) where swimmers can float?
questions.explanation: The high salt content increases density, making it easy to float.

questions.text: What percentage of the earth's surface is covered by water?
questions.explanation: This is why Earth is often called the 'Blue Planet'.

questions.text: World Water Day is celebrated on:
questions.explanation: It is observed to highlight the importance of freshwater and advocate for its sustainable management.

questions.text: When the water on the surface of the ocean rises and falls alternately, they are called:
questions.explanation: Waves are formed when winds scrape across the ocean surface.

questions.text: A huge tidal wave caused by an earthquake or volcanic eruption under the sea is a:
questions.explanation: Tsunami is a Japanese word that means 'Harbour waves'.

questions.text: The rhythmic rise and fall of ocean water twice in a day is called a:
questions.explanation: Tides are caused by the strong gravitational pull exerted by the sun and the moon.

questions.text: When water covers much of the shore by rising to its highest level, it is:
questions.explanation: High tides help in navigation and fishing.

questions.text: During the full moon and new moon days, the sun, moon and earth are in the same line and we get:
questions.explanation: These are the highest tides.

questions.text: When the moon is in its first and last quarter, the gravity of sun and moon pull in opposite directions, resulting in:
questions.explanation: Neap tides are low tides.

questions.text: Streams of water flowing constantly on the ocean surface in definite directions are:
questions.explanation: They can be warm or cold.

questions.text: The Labrador Ocean current is a ________ current.
questions.explanation: It flows from the polar regions towards lower latitudes.

questions.text: The Gulf Stream is a ________ current.
questions.explanation: Warm ocean currents originate near the equator and move towards the poles.

questions.text: Areas where warm and cold currents meet are the best ________ in the world.
questions.explanation: Examples include seas around Japan and eastern coast of North America.

questions.text: Meeting of warm and cold currents also produces ________ which makes navigation difficult.
questions.explanation: Fog reduces visibility for ships.

questions.text: Most of the water available on earth is in the form of:
questions.explanation: About 97.3% of water is saline and found in oceans and seas.

questions.text: The largest source of fresh water on earth is:
questions.explanation: Ice caps and glaciers hold about 2.0% of the total water (the largest share of fresh water).

questions.text: High tides help ships to arrive at the ________ more easily.
questions.explanation: The rising water level helps heavy ships enter ports.

questions.text: Tides are also used to generate ________ in some places.
questions.explanation: Tidal energy is a renewable source of power.

questions.text: A tsunami in the Indian Ocean in ________ caused massive destruction.
questions.explanation: It was triggered by an earthquake near Sumatra and affected many coastal countries.

questions.text: The Indira Point in ________ got submerged during the 2004 tsunami.
questions.explanation: Indira Point was the southernmost point of India.

questions.text: Warm ocean currents influence the ________ of the land.
questions.explanation: They bring about warm temperatures over the coastlines.

questions.text: The sun, moon and earth form a right angle during:
questions.explanation: The counteracting gravity leads to lower tidal ranges.

questions.text: The velocity of a tsunami wave can be more than ________ km per hour.
questions.explanation: They travel at high speeds across deep ocean waters.

questions.text: During high tide, ________ come closer to the shore, helping fishermen.
questions.explanation: This allows for a plentiful catch.

questions.text: What causes the 'rhythmic' movement of water in tides?
options: Gravitational force of moon and sun
questions.explanation: Tides are primarily a gravitational phenomenon.

questions.text: Water is a ________ resource.
questions.explanation: Even though earth is mostly water, fresh water is limited and must be conserved.

questions.text: Which of these is NOT a freshwater source?
questions.explanation: Oceans are saline.

questions.text: The height of waves depends on the:
questions.explanation: Stronger winds blow, the bigger the waves become.

questions.text: Inland seas and salt lakes account for ________ per cent of water.
questions.explanation: They contain a very small fraction of the world's water.

questions.text: Atmosphere contains water in the form of:
questions.explanation: Humidity is the measure of this water vapour.

questions.text: Water droplets in clouds fall as precipitation when they:
options: Become too heavy to float in air
questions.explanation: Gravity pulls the heavy droplets down as rain or snow.

questions.text: When the wind blows during a storm at very high speed, it forms:
questions.explanation: These can cause tremendous destruction.

questions.text: Cold ocean currents carry water from:
questions.explanation: They move water from colder to warmer regions.

questions.text: Which of these is used for indicating the direction of ocean currents?
questions.explanation: Scientists study the movement of water masses to track currents.

questions.text: The 'Indira Point' was in which island group?
questions.explanation: It was part of the Great Nicobar island.
